coredata.dc:title: Modulation of electrochemical property of carbon nanodot by post-chemical reductions
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Matsuoka, Ken-ichi
Fujita, Katsuhiko
coredata.dc:description: Influences of chemical reductions on the electrochemical properties of carbon nanodots (CNDs) were investigated. Sequential electron transfer reactions involving CNDs and several electrochromic molecules were analyzed by titration method to deduce electron accepting capabilities and upper-limit reduction potentials. CNDs prepared by alkali-assisted electrochemical oxidation of graphite were reduced by either sodium borohydride or hydrazine to obtain chemically reduced CNDs. Titration analysis of the electron transfer reactions involving methylviologen cation radicals and CNDs revealed that electron uptake per unit weight of CND increased nearly 30% after chemical reductions. Furthermore, detailed analyses of electron discharge processes involving the electron-filled CNDs and several leuco dyes provided fine estimation of upper-limit reduction potential and evidence of reduction potential shifts of CNDs to negative level by chemical reductions.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon nanodot
Electron transfer
Titration
Electrochemical property

Electron uptake behavior of CND and rCNDs from methyl viologen
Electron discharge behaviors of CND and rCND to leuco dyes

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of (a and b) CND, (c and d) rCND-A, and (e and f) rCND-B.
XPS C1s spectra of (a) CND, (b) rCND-A, and (c) rCND-B. (d) XPS N1s spectrum of rCND-B.
(a) Absorption spectra of CND/rCNDs, PL spectra of (b) CND, (c) rCND-A, and (d) rCND-B measured in ethanol.
UV–vis spectra of MV•+ solutions with incremental additions of (a) CND, (b) rCND-A (c) rCND-B. (d) Number of transferred electron versus concentration of CND/rCNDs.

Differential UV–vis spectra of electron-filled CND/rCNDs solutions followed by incremental addition of various leuco dyes. Solutions of unreduced CND/rCNDs with corresponding concentration of dyes were used as references.
Energy diagram and schematic image of the observed electron-transfer processes.
Numerical data for XPS analysis of CND, rCND-A, and rCND-B.

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: C–N component, typically appears at 285.9eV, is likely to be included.

Ratio of reduced/neutral dye at equilibrium condition and calculated
E F ∗
for each electron discharge experiments.

row.entry: [Reduced dye]/[Dye]eq. at 2.5 μM of added dye

head.ce:title: Modulation of electrochemical property of carbon nanodot by post-chemical reductions

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute for Materials Chemistry and Engineering, Kyushu University, 6-1 Kasuga Koen, Fukuoka, Kasuga 816-8580, Japan

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute for Materials Chemistry and Engineering, Kyushu University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Applied Science for Electronics and Materials, Graduate School of Engineering, Kyushu University, 6-1 Kasuga Koen, Fukuoka, Kasuga 816-8580, Japan

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Applied Science for Electronics and Materials, Graduate School of Engineering, Kyushu University

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authora at: Kyushu University, 6-1 Kasuga Koen, Kasuga, Fukuoka 816-8580, Japan. Tel.: +81 92 5837532; fax: +81 92 5837532.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Influences of chemical reductions on the electrochemical properties of carbon nanodots (CNDs) were investigated. Sequential electron transfer reactions involving CNDs and several electrochromic molecules were analyzed by titration method to deduce electron accepting capabilities and upper-limit reduction potentials. CNDs prepared by alkali-assisted electrochemical oxidation of graphite were reduced by either sodium borohydride or hydrazine to obtain chemically reduced CNDs. Titration analysis of the electron transfer reactions involving methylviologen cation radicals and CNDs revealed that electron uptake per unit weight of CND increased nearly 30% after chemical reductions. Furthermore, detailed analyses of electron discharge processes involving the electron-filled CNDs and several leuco dyes provided fine estimation of upper-limit reduction potential and evidence of reduction potential shifts of CNDs to negative level by chemical reductions.

ce:section.ce:para: Nanocarbon materials, such as fullerenes, carbon nanotubes, and graphenes, have played tremendous roles in a wide range of technological applications by efficiently accepting and transporting electrons through their sp2 carbon network [1–3]. Carbon nanodots (CNDs), or often called graphene quantum dots, newly emerged as a new class of “luminescent” nanocarbon materials with typical diameters of less than 10nm [4,5]. Rapid advance of their investigations has revealed their peculiar photoluminescence (PL) such as excitation energy-dependence [4–7,9–12], surface structure-dependence [7–11], quantum-sized effect [12], and upconversion [7,12,13]. Moreover, electrochemical properties of CNDs were found to be promising. Recent studies have demonstrated that the integration of CNDs in photovoltaic devices [14–17] and photocatalysts [12,18,19] improved efficiency. CNDs in such systems were considered to act as electron accepting units where appropriate alignment of energy levels with respect to electron donating units promoted charge separation. However, basic aspects of the electrochemical properties of CNDs are yet to be fully understood.

Modulation of redox properties of CNDs, which is crucially important for electronic applications, is one of the unexplored issues. It has been reported that PL properties of CNDs can be modulated by surface functionalization and chemical/electrochemical oxidation-reduction [7–11]. Yang et al. observed that chemical reduction of CND caused blue-shift of PL possibly due to enhanced integrity of the π conjugation system and reduced population of defect sites. Consequently, the authors proposed that both surface functionalization and chemical reductions widened the electronic bandgap of CND [7]. Seo et al. demonstrated that pristine CND prepared by exfoliation of graphite nanoparticle exhibited blue emission while chemically oxidized CND emitted green emission due to the oxygenous defects, indicating that relatively small energy gaps as compared to the intrinsic state could be created by oxidation process [11]. These studies imply that chemical functionalization, oxidation or reduction may indeed influence the redox properties of CND.

In this study, electrochemical properties of CNDs and chemically reduced CNDs (rCNDs) were investigated by means of titration method [20–23]. The effects of chemical reductions are of primary importance due to the fact that a large number of CNDs reported so far are oxidized to some extent because oxidative fragmentations of carbon sources are widely adopted for their synthetic methods. Electron storage/discharge phenomena of CNDs were quantitatively evaluated by analyzing sequential electron transfer reactions between CNDs and several electrochromic molecules. We demonstrate that chemical reductions of CNDs improve electron uptake per unite weight and slightly shifts their upper-limit reduction potential (E red) to negative level.

Methyl viologen (MV2+, Wako), thionine acetate (TH, Merck), methylene blue (MB, Fluka), oxazine 725 (OX, Exiton), nile blue A (NBA, Aldrich), and other chemicals were used as received. Graphite rod (99.9995% purity) was purchased from Alfa Aesar.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and chemical reductions of CND
ce:section.ce:para: CNDs were synthesized by the alkali-assisted electrochemical oxidation of graphite rod [12]. A pair of graphite rod was soaked into ethanol solution containing 4g/L of sodium hydroxide. Direct current (ca. 20mA/cm2) was applied between the graphite rods for 12h. The color of the electrolyte solution gradually turned into deep brown, confirming the formation of CND by electrochemical oxidation of anode graphite. The crude solution was treated with magnesium sulfate (ca. 5wt.%) for 1 day, and the solution was concentrated by rotary evaporator, after which the crude product was purified by silica-gel column chromatography with petroleum ether and diethyl ether as developing solvents to remove byproducts. The separated CNDs were highly soluble in various organic solvents such as ethanol, tetrahydrofuran (THF), toluene, and chloroform, but not soluble in water and hexane. Chemical reductions of CND by either sodium borohydride (NaBH4) or hydrazine were carried out from the same batch of CND sample. Aqueous solution of NaBH4 (0.5M, 10mL) was slowly added to the THF solution of CND (100mg in 40mL) at room temperature and stirred at room temperature overnight. In case of hydrazine reduction, 1mL of hydrazine monohydrate was slowly added to the THF solution of CND (100mg in 20mL) under nitrogen atmosphere. The solution was stirred in an oil bath (80°C) overnight. The obtained reduced CNDs were thoroughly washed with pure water and dried at 70°C in vacuo overnignt. Chemically reduced CND (rCND) samples by NaBH4 and hydrazine are denoted as rCND-A and rCND-B, respectively.

Transmission Electron Microscope (TEM) images were recorded with JEM-2100F field emission transmission electron microscope (JEOL). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were carried out by AXIS-165 (Kratos/Shimadzu) with Al Kα X-ray source. Curve fitting of XPS spectra was performed by XPS peak software (ver. 4.1). Samples for XPS measurements were prepared by casting ethanol solutions of CND/rCNDs on glass substrates covered by vacuum evaporated gold film (50nm) followed by drying under reduced pressure for at least 12h. Gold films were cleaned by piranha solution to minimize contamination just before sample preparation. UV–vis and PL spectra were recorded by UV–vis 3150 spectrometer (Shimadzu) and RF-5300PC spectrophotometer (Shimadzu), respectively.

Fig. 1 shows the TEM images of CND, rCND-A, and rCND-B. All samples were consisted of quasi-spherical nanoparticles with similar particle size. Average diameters of each samples were evaluated to be 3.6±0.9nm for CND, 3.4±0.9nm for rCND-A, and 3.6±0.9nm for rCND-B, respectively, suggesting that chemical reductions had no significant impacts on their particle size and distribution. Periodic structures with lattice spacing of 0.2 to 0.21nm were confirmed as shown in Fig. 1b,d,f. Those spacings are similar to (102) diffraction (0.208nm) and/or (104) diffractions (0.194nm) of graphitic carbon [4,7].

XPS analysis was carried out for structural characterization of CND and rCNDs as shown in Fig. 2 and Table 1 . In case of CND (Fig. 2a), the C1s spectrum was deconvoluted into five components attributable to CC (284.5eV), CC (285.5eV), CO (286.5eV), CO (287.8eV), and COO (289.2eV) species, respectively [24]. High atomic ratio of oxygen-related components in CND (53.5%) indicates the highly oxidized nature of as-prepared CND. In contrast, rCND-A and rCND-B contained significantly reduced oxygen-related components (22.5% and 19.8%, respectively). Highly reduced CO, CO, and COO components are the results chemical reductions. Consequently, atomic ratios of carbon to oxygen (C/O) estimated from C1s and O1s spectra of each sample were increased from 2.8 (CND) to 4.1 (rCND-A) and 4.7 (rCND-B), respectively. Minor amount of nitrogen was detected from rCND-B with carbon to nitrogen ratio (C/N) of 22.3 (Fig. 2d). N1s spectrum of rCND-B could be deconvoluted into two components centered at 399.4 and 400.4eV with relative intensity of 61:39 likely due to CN and C(O)N species, respectively [25]. This result implies that the nitrogen component is originated from the reaction products of CND and hydrazine.

Fig. 3a shows normalized UV–vis spectra of CND and rCNDs measured in ethanol. No drastic spectral change was observed. CND had spectral shoulder around 280nm which is attributable to n→π* transition of the CO bond [26,27]. This shoulder becomes relatively weak in cases of rCND-A and rCND-B. All samples exhibited excitation wavelength-dependent PL spectra as shown in Fig. 3b–d. Excitation by short wavelength (300nm) gave similar PL of CND, rCND-A, and rCND-B with maximum at 386nm, 389nm, and 383nm, respectively, and PL spectra of each sample red-shift along with the longer excitation wavelength. Such phenomena are commonly seen in various reports and attributable to a distribution of various emissive sites [9] and/or red-edge effect [28]. PL quantum yields (PLQY) of CND, rCND-A, rCND-B under excitation of 360nm were evaluated to be 2.6%, 6.9%, and 4.9%, respectively, by using quinine sulfate standard in 0.1M H2SO4 aqueous solution (Φ PL =54%, λ ex =360nm) [10]. Improved PLQYs after chemical reductions are most likely due to the removal of oxygen functionalities such as carboxyl groups known as PL quenching sites [7,29,30].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electron uptake behavior of CND and rCNDs from methyl viologen
ce:section.ce:para: Titration analysis was carried out to evaluate redox property of CND/rCNDs and effect of chemical reductions by probing stepwise electron transfer reactions among MV2+, CND, and several leuco dyes [21]. For titration analysis, a deaerated 0.1mM MV2+ solution in ethanol was first subjected to UV light irradiation (150W Xenon lamp, 16.5 mW/cm2) to generate MV•+. Details of the photo-reduction of MV2+ to MV•+ can be found elsewhere [31]. Reduction potential (E red) of MV2+ is −0.45V vs. NHE, and characteristic absorption bands of MV•+ around 400 and 605nm serve as optical probes for electron-transfer processes [20]. To this MV•+ solution, deaerated ethanol solutions of CND or rCNDs were introduced in small increments, and the UV–vis spectra were recorded after equilibrium was established.

As shown in Fig. 4a–c, stepwise consumption of MV•+ was observed in all cases as the concentration of CND or rCNDs increased by electron transfer from MV•+ to CND or rCNDs. Fig. 4d describes the relationship between the added amount of CND or rCNDs and concentration of transferred electrons. Number of transferred electrons was calculated from the MV•+ absorption at 607nm (ɛ =13800M−1 cm−1) [32]. Plots of Fig. 4d clearly shows that CND and rCNDs accept electrons in a quantitative manner from MV•+. Amount of stored electrons per unit weight was estimated to be 4.88μMmg−1 (CND), 6.20μMmg−1 (rCND-A), and 6.22μMmg−1 (rCND-B), respectively. These values are larger than that of graphene oxide (4.7μMmg−1) evaluated under similar conditions [22]. Electron uptake of CND increased nearly 30% after the chemical reductions. A plausible reason is the increased weight fraction of conjugated sp2 carbon sites, which is most likely responsible for accepting electrons, by the weight loss due to the removal of oxygen-related functional groups and/or evolution of new sp2 carbon site.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electron discharge behaviors of CND and rCND to leuco dyes
ce:section.ce:para: As a second part of titration analysis, electron discharge of electron-filled CND/rCNDs to secondary electron acceptors were investigated. If the consumption of MV•+ is truly associated with the electron storage in CND/rCNDs, the stored electrons will be discharged by introducing secondary electron acceptors having less negative E red than that of the stored electrons. To verify this, we employed several leuco dyes (TH, MB, OX, and NBA) as secondary electron acceptors. E red of these dyes negatively shift in the order of TH (0.064V vs. NHE)<MB (0.011V vs. NHE)<OX (−0.02V vs. NHE)<NBA (−0.119V vs. NHE) [21]. These dyes have absorption maximums around 600–650nm and undergo two-electron reduction to produce colorless products. The amount of leuco dyes reduced by the stored electrons in CND/rCNDs provides a quantitative estimate of discharged electrons.

Fig. 5a–i describe the electron discharge phenomena from electron-filled CND and rCNDs to various secondary acceptors. For electron discharge analysis, 29.0±1.2μM of electrons were initially transferred to CND or rCNDs with minimum requirements of CND or rCND dosage. Appearance of negative absorption indicates the reduction of dyes since absorbance of neutral dyes at corresponding concentration was subtracted from each spectrum. Upon addition of TH and MB to the CND solution, negative absorption appeared as these dyes were reduced by the stored electrons in CND (Fig. 5a and b). Once available electrons were titrated, further addition of dyes did not induce electron discharge. In contrast, no negative absorption appeared when OX (Fig. 5c) or NBA (data not shown) was introduced to the system. We also confirmed that TH was selectively reduced in the electron-filled CND solution with the presence of unreduced OX or NBA. These results suggested that the stored electrons in CND did not have enough potential energy to reduce OX or NBA. Based on these results, the upper-limit E red of the stored electrons in CND was estimated to lie between the E red of MB (0.011V vs. NHE) and OX (−0.020V vs. NHE).

Next, same electron discharge experiments were carried out for rCND-A and rCND-B (Fig. 5d–i). The stored electrons in both rCND-A and rCND-B were able to reduce TH and MB. Interestingly, small fraction of the stored electrons (ca. 4%) in rCND-B was further able to reduce OX (Fig. 5i). Reduction of NBA was not observed in both cases (data not shown). These results suggest that potential energy of the part of stored electrons in rCND-B exceeded E red of OX. In addition, the ratio of reduced dyes at constant dye concentration (2.5μM) is larger in the order of CND<rCND-A<rCND-B (Table 2 ). We employed Nernst equation to the titration data in order to determine the apparent Fermi level
(
E F ∗
)
of electron-filled CND and rCNDs from equation (1) [20–23]
(1)
E F ∗
=
E
Ox / Red
0
+
R T
n F
ln
[ Ox ] eq
[ Red ] eq
where
E
Ox / Red
0
is the standard E red of the secondary electron acceptors (Dye/Dye2−) and n is the stoichiomesstric number of electrons involved in the reaction (n =2). R, T, and F correspond to gas constant, temperature, and Faraday constant, respectively. The calculated results are summarized in Table 2.
E F ∗
value slightly shifts to negative potential in the order of CND<rCND-A<rCND-B which is inversely correlated with the population of oxygen-related components in XPS C1s spectra (see Table 1). Negative shift of
E F ∗
is an indication of enhanced driving force to reduce dyes most likely governed by the potential energy difference between redox objects [20,21]. Observed transition of redox properties of CNDs were attributable to the removal of oxygen functional groups such as carbonyl and carboxyl groups due to the electron withdrawing nature for aromatic compounds to positively shift LUMO level [7,33]. Removal of those groups by chemical reductions thus leads to the negative shift of E red of CNDs. Since rCND-B has the smallest population of carbonyl and carboxyl components (Table 1), it is reasonable to anticipate that the E red of rCND-B is located at the most negative level among all samples. Schematic image of the observed electron transfer events through titration analysis is shown in Fig. 6 . rCND-B contains small amount of nitrogen-related groups, which could also contribute to shift LUMO level to negative potential due to its electron donating nature. Present results suggest that chemical reductions of CNDs can modulate E red to negative level. Detailed investigations are in progress, and will be useful for the fine-tuning of redox property of CND and development of efficient electron accepting materials toward photo/electrochemical applications.

In conclusion, effects of chemical reduction on the electrochemical properties of CND were investigated by means of titration method. CND and rCNDs were able to store electrons from MV•+ and discharge them to secondary electron acceptors having appropriate E red. Nearly 30% improvement of electron uptake of CND was achieved by chemical reductions likely due to the increased weight fraction of conjugated sp2 carbon sites. In electron discharge experiments, screening of secondary electron acceptors allowed us to estimate the upper-limit E red of the stored electrons in CND/rCNDs where E red of the stored electrons in CND shifted to negative level by chemical reductions. This study demonstrated that titration method provides a versatile route for the evaluation of the electron accepting properties of CNDs.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by a Grant-in-Aid for Young Scientists (No. 24710119) from the Japan Society for the Promotion of Science (JSPS). The authors thank Dr. Yoshinori Miura (Center of Advanced Instrumental Analysis, Kyushu University) for the XPS measurements and fruitful discussions.
